model_narrative: We arrange balls in a three-by-three-by-three cube, placing exactly one ball in every cell and coloring each ball either white or black. The cube contains many straight lines of three cells running along its axes, across its faces, and through its body diagonals. A line counts against us when all three of its balls turn out to be the same color. We decide the color of every ball, and the objective is to minimize the total number of lines that end up entirely one color.

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the line-identifier symbols used to name a line by what it does along each of the three axes; the first three symbols are the three coordinate slots along an axis, while the two extra symbols mark a line that runs increasing or decreasing along that axis

sets.name: x
sets.doc: the three coordinate positions along a single axis of the cube, listed in order from first to last

sets.name: d
sets.doc: the two diagonal directions a line can take along an axis, one increasing through the positions and one decreasing

sets.name: b
sets.doc: the two sides of each line-balance relationship, a lower side and an upper side, used to bound how many balls of a color a line may hold

params.name: ls
params.doc: the sign applied to a line's ball count on each side of the balance, equal to plus one on the lower side and minus one on the upper side, so that the same line count can be bounded from both directions

params.name: lr
params.doc: the constant offset on each side of the line-balance relationship, equal to two on the lower side and minus one on the upper side

vars.name: core
vars.doc: the color placed in each cell of the three-by-three-by-three cube, indexed by its three coordinate positions; zero means a white ball and one means a black ball, with exactly one ball per cell

vars.name: line
vars.doc: a nonnegative indicator measuring how far a line is from being all one color, defined only for the symbol triples that name a genuine line of the cube

vars.name: num
vars.doc: the total count of lines whose balls all share one color

constraints.name: x)
constraints.intent: Exactly half of the cube's cells, rounded down, must hold a black ball. Summed over every cell of the cube, the number of black balls placed must equal half the total number of cells with any fraction dropped.

constraints.name: x)
constraints.intent: For each genuine line of the cube, tie its same-color indicator to the colors of the three cells it passes through, on both the lower and upper side of the balance. On the lower side, the number of black balls along the line must not exceed the line's indicator raised by the lower offset. On the upper side, the negative of that same black-ball count must not exceed the indicator lowered by the upper offset, which together force the indicator to register whenever the three cells are all black or all white. Apply this only to the symbol triples that name a real line, and skip every triple that does not correspond to an actual line of the cube.

constraints.name: x)
constraints.intent: The total count of all-one-color lines adds up the indicators of every genuine line. Set the total to the sum, over all the symbol triples that name a real line of the cube, of that line's same-color indicator.

query: We're curious what happens if the middle layer carries less weight. Hold the b-layer — every cell whose first coordinate is b — to at most three balls and let the solver redistribute the rest. What monochrome-line count does the optimizer return?
expected_pyomo: model.midlayer_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core['b', j, k] for j in model.x for k in model.x) <= 3)

query: Suppose the central plane has to stay sparse — at most two balls across the slice where the second coordinate is b. We're looking at how that thins the middle out; what's the resulting monochrome-line count?
expected_pyomo: model.centerplane_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[i, 'b', k] for i in model.x for k in model.x) <= 2)

query: We want to see the layout when the eight cube corners are all left empty — none of the (a/c, a/c, a/c) vertices may hold a ball. What does the optimizer report for the number of monochrome lines once the corners are off the table?
expected_pyomo: model.no_corners = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[i, j, k] for i in ['a', 'c'] for j in ['a', 'c'] for k in ['a', 'c']) == 0)

query: Here's a scenario we'd like to price out: the back plane — every cell whose third coordinate is b — is limited to three balls. With that depth slice capped, how many monochrome lines does the cube end up with?
expected_pyomo: model.depth_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[i, j, 'b'] for i in model.x for j in model.x) <= 3)

query: The optimal cube leans heavily on the middle b-layer. Shouldn't we keep that layer light — no more than two balls in it — so the structure isn't so center-heavy? What's the model seeing that makes it stack the middle like that?
expected_pyomo: model.midlayer_tight = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core['b', j, k] for j in model.x for k in model.x) <= 2)

query: The plan keeps planting balls right down the dead-center column — the cells where the first two coordinates are both b. Couldn't we just leave that whole central spine empty? What's pulling the optimizer to fill it?
expected_pyomo: model.spine_empty = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core['b', 'b', k] for k in model.x) == 0)

query: The bottom a-layer looks awfully crowded in the solution. Couldn't we hold that base to just two balls and spread the rest higher up? What's the tradeoff that keeps the optimizer loading the bottom?
expected_pyomo: model.base_tight = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core['a', j, k] for j in model.x for k in model.x) <= 2)
